coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot-modified TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction for boosting photocatalytic CO2 reduction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qing-shan
Yuan, Yi-chao
Li, Chu-fan
Zhang, Zhen-rui
Xia, Cheng
Pan, Wei-guo
Liu, Lu
Guo, Rui-tang
coredata.dc:description: An efficient carbon quantum dot (CQD)-modified TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction was constructed for photocatalytic CO2 reduction in this paper. Photogenerated electrons of SrTiO3 transferred to TiO2 via the transfer channel of CQDs for electron-hole recombination, while the photogenerated electrons migrated from TiO2 to CQDs to participate in the photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction. Meanwhile, the up-conversion of CQDs enhanced the light harvesting ability of the catalyst and the CQDs enriched the active reaction sites. Due to the above advantages, the CO and CH4 produce rate over 0.1-CTS reached 54.9 and 35.9 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively, which was dramatically increased compared with pure TiO2 and pure SrTiO3. This work proposes a feasible strategy for integrating CQDs with TiO2 and SrTiO3 for the optimistic cooperative effect to obtain an effective catalyst for photocatalytic CO2 reduction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
CO2 reduction
Perovskite oxides
Heterojunction
CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) and (d) TiO2, (b) and (e) SrTiO3, (c) and (f) 0.1-CTS.
(a–b) TEM images of 0.1-CTS; (c–d) HRTEM image of 0.1-CTS; (e–h) EDX mapping analysis of Sr, Ti, O, and C distribution.
(a) XRD patterns of TiO2, SrTiO3, TiO2/SrTiO3, 0.1-CTS, and 0.1-CTS after reaction; (b) FTIR spectra, (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, (d) Pore size distribution of TiO2, SrTiO3, and 0.1-CTS.
XPS spectra of TiO2, SrTiO3, and 0.1-CTS catalysts: (a) survey scan; (b) C 1s; (c) O 1s; (d) Ti 2p; (e) Sr 3d; (f) survey scan of 0.1-CTS after reaction.
(a) UV–vis DRS, (b) bandgap, and (c) VB -XPS spectra of as-fabricated catalysts.

(a) CO and CH4 evolution amount in the unit time under full-spectrum light irradiation after 7 h (b) CO2 photoreduction activities of 0.1-CTS in various conditions; Repeatability of photocatalytic CO evolution (c) and CH4 evolution (d) over 0.1-CTS under full spectrum light irradiation; Photocatalytic CO evolution (e) and CH4 evolution (f) over 0.1-CTS under NIR irradiation.
(a) PL spectra, (b) Photocurrent, (c) EIS spectra of photocatalysts, and (d) time-resolved PL decay curves.
EPR spectra of (a) aqueous dispersion (for DMPO-·O2−) and (b) methanol dispersion (for DMPO-·OH) recorded with TiO2, SrTiO3, 0.1-CTS composites.
Work functions of (a) TiO2 and (g) SrTiO3 (c) Charge transfer mode.
DOS of (a) CQDs at the middle of the heterojunction; (b) CQDs at the surface of the heterojunction.
Photocatalytic reduction mechanism of CO2 over 0.1-CTS.
Textural properties of the TiO2, SrTiO3, and 0.1-CTS composite catalysts.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot-modified TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction for boosting photocatalytic CO2 reduction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Energy and Mechanical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient carbon quantum dot (CQD)-modified TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction was constructed for photocatalytic CO2 reduction in this paper. Photogenerated electrons of SrTiO3 transferred to TiO2 via the transfer channel of CQDs for electron-hole recombination, while the photogenerated electrons migrated from TiO2 to CQDs to participate in the photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction. Meanwhile, the up-conversion of CQDs enhanced the light harvesting ability of the catalyst and the CQDs enriched the active reaction sites. Due to the above advantages, the CO and CH4 produce rate over 0.1-CTS reached 54.9 and 35.9 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively, which was dramatically increased compared with pure TiO2 and pure SrTiO3. This work proposes a feasible strategy for integrating CQDs with TiO2 and SrTiO3 for the optimistic cooperative effect to obtain an effective catalyst for photocatalytic CO2 reduction.

ce:section.ce:para: To date, the energy crisis and CO2 emissions have threatened human life [1–3]. Therefore, developing new technologies to solve the two problems is necessary. Converting carbon dioxide into value-added fuels is a solution to CO2 emissions and energy crises [4,5]. Photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide has attracted tremendous attention in the conversion of CO2 owing to its environmental friendliness and economy. The catalyst is one of the critical issues in photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Researchers have investigated various catalysts for photocatalytic CO2 reduction, such as metal oxides [6,7], metal sulfides [8,9], LDHs [10], nonmetallic materials [2,3], and so on. However, their poor utilization of solar energy of them limits their application. It is necessary to develop new photocatalysts with high activity and high utilization of solar energy for the photoreduction of CO2.

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is widely researched in the photoreduction of CO2 owing to its stability, low cost, and nontoxic property, since Halmann et al. reported the photoreduction of CO2 in 1978 [11]. However, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 is limited by the recombination of photoexcited charge carriers. Coupling TiO2 with other semiconductors (such as g-C3N4 [12], CdS [13], MoS2 [14], etc.) is one of the efficient approaches to enhance photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity. Strontium titanate (SrTiO3), with a cubic perovskite structure, has been extensively researched in the fields of photocatalytic CO2 reduction because of its suitable band alignment, flexible composition, and easy preparation [15–17]. Similar to TiO2, SrTiO3 is less efficient as a photocatalyst for the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 on its right. There are many methods to enhance the photocatalytic activity of SrTiO3, such as replacing Sr and Ti with other metals [18–20], replacing the O site with another anion [21,22], forming oxygen vacancies [23,24], and constructing heterojunctions with other semiconductors [25–28]. As a result, coupling TiO2 with SrTiO3 may be an effective strategy for improving the photocatalytic reduction of CO2, according to bandgap engineering and available scientific evidence [29–31]. The synergistic effect of TiO2 and SrTiO3 can achieve better charge separation [25,32,33], which is in favor of the photocatalytic activity for CO2 reduction. To further improve the photocatalytic performance, a third component can be introduced into the heterojunction which can enhance photogenerated electron-hole pairs and light adsorption.

CQDs (carbon quantum dots), with effective electrical conductivity and excellent optical property, consists of nanoparticles ranging in size from 2 to 10 nm [34–36]. CQDs have attracted tremendous attention of the researchers because of light-harvesting ability, acceptance of photoexcited electrons, and especially up-converting photoluminescence (PL) property. Therefore, researchers have used CQDs to modify the catalysts in the fields of photocatalytic CO2 reduction. For instance, a MoO3/CQDs/g-C3N4 composite displayed better photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity than MoO3/g-C3N4, because CQDs enhance the charge migration and light utilization [37]. In our group's previous study, CQDs was applied to modify g-C3N4/BiOIO3 and Bi12O17Cl2/NiAl-LDH heterojunctions for boosting photocatalytic CO2 reduction [1,38]. Therefore, it can be proposed that the CQDs-modified TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction maybe perform a high photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity.

In this research, the CQDs/TiO2/SrTiO3 (CTS) catalysts were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal approach to overcome the pristine flaws of TiO2 and SrTiO3, which performed a better photocatalytic performance for CO2 reduction than TiO2, SrTiO3, and TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction. This gives an idea of CO2 as a raw material to be converted into renewable energy. Moreover, the reduction mechanism was discussed based on the characterization results.

Synthesis of SrTiO
3
microspheres, TiO
2
/SrTiO
3
heterojunctions, and CQDs/TiO
2
/SrTiO
3 . SrTiO3 microspheres were fabricated by alkali hydrothermal methods [39]. 0.5 g as-prepared TiO2 and 1.67 g SrCl2·6H2O were mixed with 50 mL of DI water. Then 2 g NaOH was added to the suspension, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C in a Teflon autoclave for 6 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the sample was collected and then washed with ethanol and DI water completely. The TiO2/SrTiO3 (TS) and CQDs/TiO2/SrTiO3 (CTS) composites were synthesized by the same method, adding an excess chemical ratio of TiO2 and appropriate CQDs. The composites were denoted as x-TS and x-CTS, respectively, where x (x = 0.05, 0.1, 0.2) represented the mass percentage of TiO2. The chemicals, synthesis of TiO2 microspheres, and synthesis of CQDs are given in Supporting Information File. Specifically, all as-prepared catalysts are finally annealed in an air environment of 500 °C for 5 h to eliminate residual carbon elements in the catalyst.

Characterization and density functional theory calculation. Details of characterization experiments, photoelectrochemical characterization, and density functional theory (DFT) calculation are shown in Supporting Information File.

Photocatalytic activity evaluation. The photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity was evaluated on an MC-SPB10 system (Merry Change CO., China) under simulating sunlight with a 300W Xe lamp and a 3 a.m. 1.5 G filter. 100 mL of DI water dissolved 50 mg of photocatalysts, which was ultrasonicated for 5 min. After vacuuming the reactor, pure CO2 was introduced and held at 100 kPa for 30 min to reach the balance of adsorption-desorption, and then, the light was introduced. The suspension was continuously stirred and kept at 5 °C throughout the experimental process. The photocatalytic CO2 reduction products were analyzed by GCMS-QP2010 SE (SHIMADZU, Japan) using 13CO2 for the isotope-labeled experiment.

The apparent quantum yield (AQY) is evaluated by the following equation:
(1)
AQY
( % )
=
2 × number
of
CO + 8 × number
of
C
H 4
number
of
incident
photons
× 100 %

Micromorphology. The morphology and structure of the as-prepared catalysts were examined by SEM, TEM, HRTEM, and element mapping, as exhibited in Figs. 1 and 2
. The as-prepared TiO2 shows microsphere structures with a smooth surface, and the average size is 2–5 μm (Fig. 1a). In the process of the hydrothermal reaction, the TiO2 microsphere provides Ti atoms for preparing SrTiO3. Therefore, the obtained TiO2/SrTiO3 sample has a spherical structure composed of numerous nanospheres (TiO2) and nanocubes (SrTiO3), as shown in Fig. 1b and e. For 0.1-CTS, it is observed that the TiO2 nanospheres and SrTiO3 nanocubes form the microspheres (Fig. 1c and f), confirming the successful preparation of TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction. There are no CQDs detected on the SEM images, due to their small size. That is, the addition of CQDs does not affect the morphology of the TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction. The microstructure of 0.1-CTS was characterized by TEM. Fig. 2 shows the TEM image of 0.1-CTS, demonstrating the nanocube structure of SrTiO3 and the nanosphere structure of TiO2 successfully constructing heterojunction, which is beneficial to charge transfer. Furthermore, many black quantum dots can be observed in Fig. 2a and b. As illustrated in Fig. 2, it is observed that the lattice fringes spacing of 0.22, 0.275, and 0.31 nm is ascribed to SrTiO3 (111), SrTiO3 (110), and TiO2 (110), respectively. Additionally, C, Sr, Ti, and O elements can be observed in 0.1-CTS by element mapping (Fig. 2d–g). The above-observed morphologies demonstrate the intimate fusion of CQDs, TiO2, and SrTiO3.

Crystal structure. The crystal structures and phase purity of as-fabricated catalysts, as shown in Fig. 3 a, are measured by XRD. The XRD peaks of TiO2 at 25.28, 37.80, 47.86, 54.26, and 62.76° correspond with (101), (004), (200), (105), and (204) plane of typical anatase TiO2 (PDF#21–1272). The XRD peaks of SrTiO3 at 32.28, 39.82, 46.34, 57.66, 67.66, and 77.04° are ascribable to the (110), (111), (200), (211), (220), and (310) planes of SrTiO3 (PDF#35–0734) [40], separately. Meanwhile, the (110) and (111) crystal planes of SrTiO3 are consistent within HRTEM. In the heterojunctions of TiO2/SrTiO3 and 0.1-CTS, the XRD peaks of TiO2 and SrTiO3 were obviously observed, indicating the successful recombination of TiO2 and SrTiO3. After the construction of the heterojunction, the XRD peaks of SrTiO3 slightly shift to smaller degree values, due to the lattice expansion, which is caused by extration of Ti4+ cation from TiO2 to SrTiO3 [41]. The needle-like peaks of the as-prepared composites show that they have good crystallinity. The incorporation of CQDs reduces the intensity of diffraction peaks compared to the TiO2/SrTiO3, which might be due to the in-situ loading of CQDs on the catalysts. Meanwhile, there are no other changes in the XRD patterns of TiO2/SrTiO3 and 0.1-CTS, and the peaks of CQDs are not found in the XRD pattern of 0.1-CTS, owing to the low content and amorphous structure of CQDs.

Fig. 3b shows the FTIR spectra of 0.1-CTS, SrTiO3, and TiO2 samples. In the spectra of all samples, the low-frequency bands centered at 500-900 cm−1 are metal-oxygen stretchings, such as Ti–O and Sr–O bands [42]. The absorption bands at around 1471 and 1603 cm−1 are attributed to C–O stretching vibration [43]. From the spectrum of SrTiO3, we find that there are carbon residues during the preparation of SrTiO3. The results of the FTIR similarly show the successful synthesis of the ternary complex. The BET was measured to detect the textural properties of samples. As shown in Fig. 3c, all catalysts perform the type Ⅳ isotherms, demonstrating the mesoporous structure exhibited in the compounds. Table 1 shows the specific surface areas and pore sizes of 0.1-CTS, pure SrTiO3, and pure TiO2. It is obvious that the ternary complex could improve the BET surface area (55.5 m2/g), compared with the pure SrTiO3 (14.716 m2/g) and pure TiO2 (13.346 m2/g). Having a larger specific surface area can result in an increase of active sites available for reactions. The BJH pore size distributions of TiO2, SrTiO3, and 0.1-CTS compounds (Fig. 3d) deflect the narrow pore size distribution ranging from 3.0 to 4.3 nm.

Chemical composition. The elemental compositions and chemical states of TiO2, SrTiO3, and 0.1-CTS were examined by XPS measurements, which are shown in Fig. 4 . All elements (C, O, Ti, Sr) are detected in the full spectrum of 0.1-CTS. The C 1s peak at 289.5 eV of 0.1-CTS is ascribed to CO, owing to the introduction of CQDs [44]. Furthermore, there are no peaks of C connected with other elements, and it is proved that the C and O species exist in a stable interaction on the catalyst surface. The corresponding O 1s XPS peaks are shown in Fig. 1c, indicating that the heterojunction and CQDs greatly change the states of oxygen on the catalyst surface. The O 1s peak at 529.2 eV of SrTiO3 is ascribed to the lattice oxygen of SrTiO3 and the O 1s peak at 530.5 eV of TiO2 is owing to the Ti–O bond. While the O 1s peaks on sides at 532 eV of all catalysts are attributed to chemisorbed O/O−/vacancies species [45]. After constructing heterojunction and CQDs modifying, the lattice oxygen of SrTiO3 is not detected in the XPS spectrum, further proving that the CQDs forms a CO bond with the lattice oxygen of SrTiO3, which is in accordance with the C 1s peak at 289.5 eV. As exhibited in Fig. 4d, the Ti 2p peaks are assigned at two positions, which are attributed to Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2, separately, and the binding energy of Ti 2p of TiO2 is much higher than that of SrTiO3 and 0.1-CTS, which is attributed to the Sr and C elements introduction to TiO2 and the pure TiO2 phase. Moreover, the peaks of Ti 2p of 0.1-CTS are slightly lower than that of SrTiO3, demonstrating that the introduction of CQDs causes the Ti element in the heterojunction to obtain electrons and reduce the valence state (Ti4+→Ti3+). The Ti3+ of SrTiO3/TiO2 heterojunction plays a vital role in photocatalytic CO2 reduction [46]. The Sr 3d spectrum of SrTiO3 is fitted with two peaks at 132.5 and 134.2 eV, attributed to Sr 3d5/2 and Sr 3d3/2, respectively. The Sr 3d peaks of 0.1-CTS are shifted to the augmented region (from 132.5 to 134.2 eV to 132.7 and 134.4 eV, respectively), which indicates that the Sr element loses electrons in 0.1-CTS composites. All the changes in binding energy show that the heterojunction is formed with TiO2, SrTiO3, and CQDs.

Optical properties. The light absorption characteristic of the catalysts is essential to research the activity of photocatalytic CO2 reduction. The UV–vis DRS of the fabricated catalysts is illustrated in Fig. 5 a, revealing the light absorption ability of the photocatalysts. The adsorption edge of pure TiO2 and SrTiO3 is approximately 400 nm and 420 nm, respectively. Compared to their edges, the adsorption edge of the TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction is red-shifted to 455 nm, which may be attributed to the close contact between TiO2 and SrTiO3. After the incorporation of CQDs, the 0.1-CTS obviously performs improved light adsorption in the visible light and near-infrared regions. Moreover, the adsorption values of 0.1-CTS appear stronger than that of TiO2, SrTiO3, and TiO2/SrTiO3 based on the vertical coordinates, which is useful to facilitate photoreduction and light harvesting. This may be due to the unique up-conversion properties of CQDs, which can convert long wavelengths of light into photoluminescence [1]. Besides, the band gap of TiO2 and SrTiO3 can be estimated by the formula αhv = A (hv - E
g )n/2, where α, h, v, A, and Eg are the absorption coefficient, Plank's constant, the light frequency, a constant according to the catalyst and the bandgap energy, separately. The value of n is controlled by its direct or indirect transition semiconductor type. Regarding the indirect transition catalyst of TiO2 and SrTiO3, the value of n for them is 4. Therefore, the bandgaps of pure TiO2 and SrTiO3 are 2.91 and 3.05 eV, respectively, which are shown in Fig. 5b. Furthermore, the VB-XPS were explored to estimate the position of the VB maximum (Fig. 5c). According to the results of VB-XPS and the bandgaps, the VB and CB of TiO2 are calculated to be 2.28 eV and −0.63 eV (vs. NHE); the VB and CB of SrTiO3 were calculated to be 1.39 eV and −1.66 eV (vs. NHE), severally.

Evaluation of photocatalytic activity. The performances of photocatalytic CO2 reduction of the as-fabricated samples are shown in Fig. 6 . In the controlled experiments (Fig. 6b), no carbon compounds were detected when there was no CO2, catalyst, or light, suggesting they all played a vital part in photocatalytic CO2 reduction. As displayed in Fig. 5a, pristine TiO2 displays a low activity of CO2 reduction with yields of 3.2 and 4.4 μmol g−1 h−1 for CO and CH4 under irradiation after 7 h, respectively, as well as the pure SrTiO3, with yields of 2.2 and 0 μmol g−1 h−1, severally. Of note is that after the addition of Sr to form SrTiO3, its activity of CO2 reduction decreases, which is probably because the as-prepared SrTiO3 has a larger bandgap than TiO2. The TiO2/SrTiO3 composite shows optical photocatalytic activity under the same conditions with yields of 10.5 and 7.5 μmol g−1 h−1 for CO and CH4, respectively, owing to the synergistic effects of TiO2 and SrTiO3 enhancing the charge separation [29]. Compared with the pure SrTiO3, the C–SrTiO3 composite shows high activity for CO2 reduction with yields of 23.3 and 6.48 μmol g−1 h−1 of CO and CH4, respectively, which is probably because the incorporation of CQDs enhances the light-harvesting and supplies more active sites to the reaction. When the CQDs, TiO2, and SrTiO3 are compounded by the in-situ hydrothermal method, CO and CH4 yields obviously increase, indicating the CQDs modified heterojunction structure is in favor of the CO2 photoreduction. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, the yields of CO and CH4 of the ternary complexes were significantly higher than those of the monomeric and binary complexes. The optimal ratio of the ternary complex is 0.1-CTS, which achieves maximum yields of 54.9 and 35.9 μmol g−1 h−1 of CO and CH4, respectively. Meanwhile, the electron selectivity of methane over 0.1-CTS catalyst reached 72.3 %. The CO yields of 0.1-CTS are roughly 17.2, 25, 5.2, and 2.4 times higher than that of pure TiO2, pure SrTiO3, TiO2/SrTiO3, and C–SrTiO3, respectively. The CH4 yields of 0.1-CTS are roughly 8.2, 4.8, and 5.5 times higher than that of pure TiO2, TiO2/SrTiO3, and C–SrTiO3, respectively. Further increase of TiO2 is not beneficial for the performance of CO2 photoreduction, probably because of the excessive TiO2 occupying the active sites on the microspheres or CQDs. In Table S1, the performance of 0.1-CTS catalyst compared with that of photocatalytic CO2 reduction to CO and CH4 reported in other literature. The apparent quantum yield of 0.1-CTS is 3.33 % at 420 nm. Moreover, O2 is also detected in the reaction.

We know that CQDs have up-conversion characteristics, so it is necessary to investigate their effect on CO2 reduction. Under near-infrared spectra (NIR) irradiation, the activity of CO2 photoreduction of the as-synthesized catalysts was explored. Only the complexes containing CQDs exhibit CO2 reduction activity under NIR irradiation, while the others show no product. Among them, the yields of CO and CH4 of the 0.1-CTS composite achieve 5.4 and 3.5 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively after NIR irradiation for 7 h, as illustrated in Fig. 6e-f. The photocatalytic activity results indicate that the CQDs can enhance the light-harvesting ability of the photocatalyst ascribed to the up-conversion characteristic of CQDs. Consequently, the incorporation of CQDs into the catalyst can boost both the synergy between TiO2, CQDs, and SrTiO3, as well as the light utilization of the photocatalyst. This, in turn, enhances the photocatalytic performance of CO2 reduction.

Moreover, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) was applied to verify the sources of carbon of the products in the process of photocatalytic CO2 reduction. As shown in the inset figure of Fig. 6b, the 13CO (m/z = 29) and 13CH4 (m/z = 17) are detected by mass spectrometry analysis in the photoreduction of 13CO2 (m/z = 45), proving that the products of photocatalytic reactions come from CO2 reduction. To research the stability of the as-prepared samples, three cycles of the photocatalytic CO2 reduction were explored, showing the yields of CO and CH4 over 0.1-CTS without notable changes after 21 h (Fig. 6c-d). These results prove that the production of CO and CH4 products can be attributed to the process of photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Furthermore, the XRD and XPS of the 0.1-CTS after the reaction are displayed in Figs. 3 and 4f. Compared to the fresh 0.1-CTS, the crystal structure and surface element valence of the 0.1-CTS after reaction do not significantly vary, which further demonstrate that the heterojunction has remarkable stability.

Photocatalytic mechanism. To research the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the 0.1-CTS, we performed photoluminescence (PL) measurements to investigate the efficacy of its photoinduced charge separation. Generally, As displayed in Fig. 7 a, all the as-prepared catalysts exhibit a wide emission band at 350–450 nm. The PL emission intensity of the pure TiO2 and SrTiO3 is stronger than that of the TiO2/SrTiO3, indicating that the heterojunction suppresses the recombination of the photogenerated charge pairs. Furthermore, after the incorporation of the CQDs, the PL intensity of 0.1-CTS is decreased than the TiO2/SrTiO3 heterojunction. It is probably because CQDs act as electron transport channels in the heterojunction, suppressing electron-hole pairs recombination. Therefore, the reduction of the PL intensity further indicates the heterojunction is constructed and the CQDs act as electron mediators between TiO2 and SrTiO3 or electron receivers on the surface of the catalyst, which all suppressed the photoinduced charge recombination.

Transient photocurrent response (TPR) was explored to research the charge separation properties of the photocatalysts, which is shown in Fig. 7b. Compared to pure TiO2 and SrTiO3, the TiO2/SrTiO3 exhibits a higher transient photocurrent intensity, suggesting that the heterojunction is in favor of the charge separation. Furthermore, the 0.1-CTS shows the strongest photocurrent intensity among the as-prepared samples, indicating that the incorporation of CQDs generates more electron-hole pairs. The results of the TPR are consistent with the PL intensity. Besides, the electron transfer resistances of the as-prepared samples were explored by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As illustrated in Fig. 7c, the 0.1-CTS composite has the smallest EIS arc radius compared to the heterojunction and pure monomers, indicating the lowest electron transfer resistance, which is also accompanied by the PL spectra. The TPR and EIS results suggest the heterojunctions and CQDs incorporation improve the electron-hole pairs separation and alleviate the electron transfer resistance. Moreover, the lifetimes (τ) of the light-induced carriers were explored by time-resolved PL decay spectroscopy. The TS and 0.1-CTS decay curves were illustrated in Fig. 7d and the τ values are exhibited in the insert table of Fig. 7d. The τ value could be determined using Equation (2) [1]:
(2)
τ =
A 1
τ 1
2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A
2
τ 2
where the B1 and B2 are the constants, and τ1 and τ2 are the fast and slow decay components, respectively. The τ values of TiO2/SrTiO3 and 0.1-CTS are 52.5 and 28.5 ns, respectively, indicating the incorporation of CQDs enhances the electron-hole pairs separation and charge migration [41]. Therefore, the heterojunction and introduction of CQDs could enhance the charge generation, promote the charge transfer, and suppress the electron-hole pairs recombination, thereby favoring the performance of the photocatalytic CO2 reduction.

The EPR technique was investigated to research the charge migration mechanism, which used 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a trapping agent to produce DMPO-·O2 − and DMPO-·OH to evaluate ·O2 − and ·OH production, separately. As seen in Fig. 8 , there are no detections of EPR signals (·O2 − and ·OH) for 0.1-CTS in the absence of light. Under visible light, TiO2, SrTiO3, and 0.1-CTS all show obvious signals of O2 − and ·OH, demonstrating that all as-prepared samples have the redox ability. Notably, the signals of O2 − and ·OH of 0.1-CTS are stronger than pure TiO2 and SrTiO3, indicating that the heterojunction and the incorporation of CQDs enhance the separation of electron-hole pairs. More electron-hole pairs can be involved in the redox reactions, leading to enhanced redox capacities and the enhanced ability of photocatalytic CO2 reduction.

Based on the previous UV and VB-XPS test results, we have plotted the conduction and valence band diagrams of TiO2 and SrTiO3, which are shown in Fig. 9 c. To detect the charge transfer between SrTiO3 and TiO2, their work functions (WFs) were calculated by DFT. As shown in Fig. 9a-b, the work functions of SrTiO3(001) and TiO2(001) are 3.92 eV and 3.76 eV, indicating the internal electric field flow from TiO2 to SrTiO3 after the two materials come to contact until their Fermi levels (FL) are the same and the electrons flow in the opposite direction, indicating the migration of electrons from SrTiO3 to TiO2. Meanwhile, the heterojunction of TiO2 and SrTiO3 was calculated by DFT. The results of DFT show that the TiO2 and SrTiO3 construct intimate heterojunction at the interface, confirming that the two materials are easy to construct heterojunctions. The Mulliken atomic charges of TiO2(001), SrTiO3(001), and heterojunction are listed in Table S1. After constructing a heterojunction, the Ti element of TiO2 gains electrons, causing a decrease in the valence state of Ti atoms of TiO2. On the other hand, the Sr atoms of SrTiO3 lose electrons leading to an increase in the valence state of Sr atoms of SrTiO3. These results are in accordance with the XPS characteristic. As labeled in Table S2, the label 1–40 atoms belong to TiO2, and all Mulliken atomic charges sum up to −0.154, further indicating that TiO2 gains electrons and SrTiO3 loses electrons in the heterojunction. Therefore, it is proposed that the as-prepared ternary composite catalyst abides by the Ⅱ-scheme heterojunction in the photocatalytic reduction of CO2. To further research the function of the CQDs in the catalysts, the DOS of CTS heterojunctions were calculated. As shown in Fig. 10 , CQDs serve as electron transport channels in the middle of heterojunctions (Fig. 10a), while they serve as electron acceptors on the surface of heterojunctions (Fig. 10b). Likewise, the Mulliken atomic charges of CTS are listed in Table S3. The results suggest the CQDs enhance the charge migration in comparison to binary composite. The Ti atoms of TiO2 gain more electrons than binary composite, which is conducive to the photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction. Therefore, the incorporation of CQDs improves the charge transfer and electron-hole pairs separation.

To sum up, the probably photocatalytic CO2 reduction mechanism of CTS can be proposed in Scheme 1 . Under light irradiation, 0.1-CTS is photoexcited and the electrons of VB transfer to their CB. The microspheres composed of nanocubes and nanospheres are beneficial for the adsorption of CO2 molecules on the surface of 0.1-CTS and CQDs can provide more active reaction sites for photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Meanwhile, the up-conversion of CQDs improves the light harvesting ability of the catalyst, enhancing the utilization of light. Between TiO2 and SrTiO3, the CQDs serve as the bridge for electrons, accelerating the charge separation of TiO2 and SrTiO3 and suppressing the charge recombination of pure semiconductor. Meanwhile, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of TiO2 with strong reduction ability are gathered on the CQDs and involved in the reaction of CO2 reduction, which reduces CO2 to chemical fuels. Simultaneously, the holes on the VB of SrTiO3 with strong oxidation ability involve in the oxidate reaction, which oxidates H2O to O2 or H2O2. Due to the construction of Ⅱ-scheme heterostructures and CQDs serving as electron mediators and acceptors, the 0.1-CTS nanocatalyst performed a high photocatalytic CO2 reduction ability.

In conclusion, a 0.1-CTS compound material was successfully prepared by a hydrothermal approach. Confirmation of the loading of CQDs onto the microspheres and the formation of TiO2/SrTiO3 heterostructures with close contacts were achieved through morphology and structural analysis. Compared to pure TiO2 and SrTiO3, 0.1-CTS performed an excellent photocatalytic CO2 reduction ability under simulated sunlight irradiation. The significant improvement is primarily ascribed to the electron reservoir and mediator properties and up-conversion fluorescence of the CQDs, which significantly enhance electron-hole pairs separation and boost the light harvesting ability. Simultaneously, the CQDs induce the increase of Ti3+ content in the ternary catalyst, which is conducive to photocatalytic CO2 reduction. To sum up, this work constructed a Ⅱ-scheme heterostructured photocatalyst by the synergistic effects of electron reservoir and electron transport channel of CQDs, which can be employed in photocatalytic CO2 reduction.

Qing-shan Wang: Writing – original draft. Yi-chao Yuan: Supervision. Chu-fan Li: Investigation. Zhen-rui Zhang: Investigation. Cheng Xia: Writing – review & editing. Wei-guo Pan: Supervision. Lu Liu: Methodology. Rui-tang Guo: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was sponsored by Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (23ZR1424500).
